Welcome to the documentation for the Critical Path Method (CPM) Project Management Platform. This system combines a high-performance compiled C++ scheduling engine with a real-time collaborative Next.js web application. info This platform is designed for projects with complex, nested dependencies where date changes trigger scheduling impacts throughout the network.

Standard project management systems (like Trello or Jira) excel at tracking flat task backlogs but struggle with complex dependency structures: Changes in one task's duration have silent, compounding impacts on downstream dependencies. Identifying the critical path (the sequence of dependent tasks determining the minimum project duration) is difficult to compute on the fly. Performing full scheduling passes over thousands of tasks is computationally expensive in standard JavaScript runtimes.

Our scheduling platform solves these problems through: Native C++ Performance: A compiled scheduling engine (cpm_cli) runs topological sorts and float calculations in microseconds. Interactive Node Layouts: Render dependency networks using React Flow (@xyflow/react) with dynamic layout positioning. Chronological Gantt Timelines: Drag-and-drop timeline bars, filter weekends, and highlight scheduling float times. Real-time Sync: Keep all team members synchronized instantly via a persistent WebSocket bridge.

Explore the system design and developer guides in the following sections: System Architecture — Layout of component boundaries and IPC loops. Technology Stack — Deep-dive into technical decisions and framework dependencies. Project Structure — File directory layout. Frontend Application — React components, React Flow canvas, and custom Gantt schedulers. Backend Integration — Next.js routing, child-process spawner, and WebSocket event bridge. Database Schema — Entity relations, unique keys, and volume benchmarks. CPM Engine Reference — Algorithmic implementation, math formulas, and JavaScript fallbacks. System Workflows — Sequence maps for logins, schedule computations, and XML imports. API Reference Guide — Route parameters, request payloads, and status codes. Authentication & Roles — Argon2 password hashing, JWT sessions, and RBAC policies. State Management — Local Zustand variables, React Query caches, and socket syncs. Deployment Guide — CMake compiler targets, environment flags, and PaaS hosting. Development Guide — Running tests, local seeding, and volume stresses. Glossary — Key project management and algebraic terminology.
This document lists all the REST API endpoints provided by the CPM platform backend, including methods, URLs, query parameters, payloads, and authorization requirements.

Method Endpoint Description Auth Level Payload (JSON) Response (JSON) POST /api/v1/auth/signup Register a new user Public {name, email, password} {user: {id, name, email}} POST /api/v1/auth/login Login user, set session cookie Public {email, password} {accessToken, refreshToken, user} POST /api/v1/auth/logout Logout user, clear cookies Public None {status: "ok"} POST /api/v1/auth/token Get current access token Authenticated None {accessToken} POST /api/v1/auth/refresh Rotate session refresh tokens Authenticated None {accessToken, refreshToken}

Method Endpoint Description Auth Level Payload (JSON) Response (JSON) GET /api/v1/users/me Fetch active profile Session Cookie None {id, name, email} PUT /api/v1/users/me Update name or avatar Session Cookie {name, avatar} {id, name, email, avatar} PUT /api/v1/users/me/password Change user password Session Cookie {oldPassword, newPassword} {status: "success"}

Method Endpoint Description Auth Level Payload (JSON) Response (JSON) GET /api/v1/projects List all user projects Session Cookie None [{id, name, identifier, status}] POST /api/v1/projects Create a new project Workspace Admin {name, identifier, description} {id, name, identifier} GET /api/v1/projects/[projectId] Fetch project detail Project Member None {id, name, identifier, tasks: []} PUT /api/v1/projects/[projectId] Update project metadata Project Admin {name, status, health} {id, name, status, health} DELETE /api/v1/projects/[projectId] Delete a project (cascade) Project Owner None {status: "deleted"} GET /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/overview Fetch cached dashboard KPIs Project Member None {project: {}, cpmInsights: {}, schedule: {}}

Method Endpoint Description Auth Level Payload (JSON) Response (JSON) GET /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/tasks List all project tasks Project Member None [{id, title, duration, state}] POST /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/tasks Create a new task Project Member {title, duration, state} {id, title, duration} PUT /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/tasks/[taskId] Update task details Project Member {title, duration, state, startDate, endDate} {id, title, duration, state} DELETE /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/tasks/[taskId] Soft-delete a task Project Member None {status: "deleted"} POST /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/tasks/bulk-import Bulk XML parsing import Project Member {xmlContent} {importedTasksCount: 12} GET /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/dependencies List project dependencies Project Member None [{id, predecessorTaskId, successorTaskId, type}] POST /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/dependencies Create dependency link Project Member {predecessorTaskId, successorTaskId, type, lag} {id, predecessorTaskId, successorTaskId} DELETE /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/dependencies/[dependencyId] Delete dependency link Project Member None {status: "deleted"}

Endpoint: /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/cpm/run Method: POST Role: Project Member (Write Access) Description: Triggers topological sorting and dates computation. If the C++ process is compiled and available, it spawns cpm_cli. Otherwise, it falls back to the in-memory JavaScript solver. Request Payload: None Response Payloads: Status: 202 Accepted { "runId": "c4b4a11f-db9f-4318-8f81-a67bde56a1b2", "status": "processing" } Status: 400 Bad Request (Validation failure / Cycles) { "error": "CPM Engine Validation Failed: Cycle detected: task A -> task B -> task A" }

Endpoint: /api/v1/projects/[projectId]/cpm/results Method: GET Role: Project Member (Read Access) Description: Returns the latest calculated snapshot results containing task dates, floats, and the critical path. Response Payload: Status: 200 OK { "id": "c4b4a11f-db9f-4318-8f81-a67bde56a1b2", "projectDuration": 24.5, "criticalPath": ["task-1", "task-3", "task-4"], "details": { "status": "success", "taskDetails": { "task-1": { "es": 0.0, "ef": 5.0, "ls": 0.0, "lf": 5.0, "slack": 0.0, "isCritical": true }, "task-2": { "es": 5.0, "ef": 12.0, "ls": 8.0, "lf": 15.0, "slack": 3.0, "isCritical": false }, "task-3": { "es": 5.0, "ef": 15.0, "ls": 5.0, "lf": 15.0, "slack": 0.0, "isCritical": true }, "task-4": { "es": 15.0, "ef": 24.5, "ls": 15.0, "lf": 24.5, "slack": 0.0, "isCritical": true } } } } Status: 404 Not Found (if no scheduling run has been executed yet) { "error": "No CPM snapshot found for this project" }
This document details the system design, modular boundaries, and communication flows of the CPM Project Management platform.

The system uses a mixed architectural model: a standard Three-Tier Web Architecture (Presentation, Application, Database) coupled with a Native Computation Worker Pattern for schedule calculations, and a Publish-Subscribe Event Bridge for real-time collaboration. note Running the scheduling engine as a short-lived process prevents memory leaks and isolates CPU-intensive graph operations from the Node.js event loop.

Technology: Next.js 16 (Client Components), React Flow (@xyflow/react), Zustand, Tailwind CSS v4. Responsibility: Renders the scheduling dashboard, Gantt timeline, and task dependency graph. Handles user interactions such as dragging nodes, editing details, bulk importing XML files, and configuring automation modes. State Management: Consumes API endpoints via TanStack React Query. Maintains workspace routing state in Zustand (workspaceStore.ts). Consumes WebSocket events to perform optimistic UI updates and queries refetching.

Technology: Node.js, Next.js API Routes (route.ts), custom HTTP wrapper (server.mjs). Responsibility: Hosts static assets, runs Server Side rendering, serves REST API endpoints, performs user authentication, manages role-based access controls (RBAC), and manages transactions on the database. Integration Bridge: Converts Prisma database models into Graph DTO payloads, spawns the C++ binary via Node's child_process.spawnSync, handles exit codes, and saves results back to the database as snapshots.

Technology: Node.js ws library, custom bridge (ws-server.mjs and ws-emitter.ts). Responsibility: Maintains active TCP connections with logged-in users. Places clients into project rooms (project:${projectId}) after verifying their access tokens (JWT) and database project memberships. HTTP Bridge: Exposes a secure /emit POST endpoint. This allows Next.js API routes (which run in transient server environments) to broadcast events to connected clients by sending a payload to the WebSocket server's HTTP bridge.

Technology: C++17, compiled via CMake to cpm_cli. Responsibility: Accepts JSON input from stdin, runs Kahn's topological sort, detects cycles, calculates scheduling passes, computes floats, extracts critical paths using Depth-First Search (DFS), and prints JSON output to stdout.
documents.s: D. Native Computation Worker (C++ CPM CLI)

documents.t: Technology: PostgreSQL, Prisma ORM, Redis (ioredis with in-memory fallback). Responsibility: Stores persistent state. Uses Redis for api-response caching (query-cache, activity-cache, project-overview-cache) and rate limiting. Falls back to a local in-memory mock store if Redis is unavailable.

The platform relies on four communication styles depending on performance requirements: Pattern Protocol Source Destination Purpose Request-Response HTTPS (JSON REST) Browser Next.js API User actions, CRUD operations, configuration. Local Inter-process (IPC) Stdin/Stdout Pipes Next.js Server C++ cpm_cli Performing scheduling calculations. Publish-Subscribe WebSockets (WS) WebSocket Server Browser Distributing real-time updates and notifications. Server-to-Server Bridge HTTP POST Next.js Server WebSocket Server Initiating real-time broadcasts from REST endpoints.
Here is the sequential flow of scheduling runs:

To maintain testability, dependencies only flow inwards: Routing & Delivery Layer (apps/web/src/app): Decouples request routing, body parsing, and status code delivery from the underlying data services. Data Repository Layer (src/database/repositories.ts): Wraps the Prisma client. No business logic knows how database queries are constructed; they invoke named repository functions (e.g., listProjectTasks). Caching Layer (apps/web/src/lib/*-cache.ts): Wraps database queries. It manages cache keys, TTL values, and cache invalidation policies, returning data without calling the database if a cache entry is hit. Integration Layer (src/integration/cpmService.ts): Maps database models to the JSON structures expected by the C++ engine. It isolates the IPC child_process call, translating low-level shell execution codes into standard TypeScript Error classes.
This document describes the security architecture of the CPM platform, covering password cryptography, JWT session management, cookie policies, and granular Role-Based Access Controls (RBAC).

To protect against credential leaks, the database does not store plain text passwords: Hashing Algorithm: Argon2id (using the argon2 Node package). Process: During signup, the server hashes the user's password using a random salt and stores the resulting hash in the passwordHash field of the User table. Verification: During login, the server matches the provided password against the stored hash using verify(user.passwordHash, password).
The platform uses a stateless, token-based session management system stored in secure client cookies.

Upon successful login, two JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) are signed using jose and set in the user's browser cookies: accessToken: Extends session access. refreshToken: Extends session renewal. warning Both cookies utilize the HttpOnly flag to prevent access from client-side JS scripts (XSS mitigation), and the Secure flag to enforce transmission over HTTPS. Both cookies are configured with the following flags to prevent scripting attacks and eavesdropping: HttpOnly: true (prevents client-side scripts like document.cookie from reading the token, mitigating XSS risks). Secure: true (ensured in production environments so cookies are sent only over HTTPS). Path: / (makes cookies available across all subroutes of the application). Max-Age: 7 days (session duration matches the token expiration date).

For server-rendered components, the helper function getSession() in apps/web/src/lib/auth.ts extracts and validates the access token: JWT Verification: Decodes the token using the system JWT_SECRET. Deduplication Cache: Wrapped in React's cache() function, this utility dedupes database validation checks. If multiple nested server components in a single render tree invoke getSession(), the JWT decoding and database lookup run exactly once, optimizing request latency.
Access levels are structured into two hierarchical tiers: Workspace Roles and Project Roles.

Workspace roles determine general administrative control over project directories, billing, and team memberships: Role Name Description / Permissions OWNER Full root administrative access. Can delete the workspace, rename slugs, invite members, and change billing settings. ADMIN Can create projects, invite workspace members, delete any project, and define custom project roles. MEMBER Can view public projects and join invited project teams. Cannot create workspace-level roles. VIEWER Read-only access to workspace statistics. Cannot modify any workspace metadata.
Inside a project, access control is managed by project-specific membership roles and custom permission sets defined in the database:

Role Name Description / Permissions PROJECT OWNER Full control over the project. Can delete the project, run scheduling runs, and modify memberships. PROJECT ADMIN Can create, edit, and delete tasks and dependencies. Can invite members to the project. PROJECT MEMBER Can create and edit tasks. Can trigger CPM runs. Can create project comments. PROJECT VIEWER Read-only access to tasks, Gantt views, and graphs. Cannot trigger scheduling runs.

For complex teams, administrators can define custom roles with granular permission flags stored in the database: canCreateTask (boolean) canDeleteTask (boolean) canManageDependencies (boolean) canTriggerCpm (boolean) canPostAnnouncements (boolean) canManageMembers (boolean) These permissions are checked by Next.js API routes before performing database writes, returning a 403 Forbidden error if the user's role lacks the required permissions.
This document details the backend design of the CPM platform, outlining API routes, custom WebSocket servers, caching policies, compiled C++ integration, and backend request lifecycles.

The backend is composed of two servers running concurrently in a Node.js runtime: Next.js API Server (server.mjs): Listens on port 3000. It hosts client routes, server actions, and REST API controllers. WebSocket Server (ws-server.mjs): Listens on port 3001 in standalone mode, or hooks directly into port 3000 in unified mode. It handles persistent TCP connections and room-based broadcasts.

Business logic is organized into mock controllers located in Next.js API endpoints (apps/web/src/app/api/v1/), which interact with database repositories (src/database/repositories.ts): Auth Service: Manages login/signup. Hashes passwords using argon2 and signs cookie-based JWT sessions using jose. Project Service: Manages projects, tags, departments, and custom roles. Task & Dependency Service: Coordinates CRUD operations, validations, and bulk updates. CPM Integration Service: Orchestrates pipeline execution by spawning the C++ scheduler.
The backend interfaces with the native compiled C++ CLI executable (build/cpm_cli) using a spawned process model:

Command: spawnSync(join(process.cwd(), 'build', 'cpm_cli'), [], { input: JSON.stringify(payload), encoding: 'utf-8', maxBuffer: 50MB }). Data Exchange: Inputs are piped directly to the child process's stdin as a stringified JSON graph. The executable returns results to stdout or writes validation errors to stderr. Timeout and Buffers: A maxBuffer of 50MB is allocated to accommodate large graphs (up to 5,000 tasks). Error Mapping: If the process exits with a non-zero code, cpmService.ts parses stderr (e.g. {"error": "Cycle detected: A -> B -> A"}) and throws a CpmIntegrationError. This is caught by API controllers to return clean client error responses.

Because Next.js App Router API routes execute in transient, isolated request threads, they cannot directly access client TCP sockets. The platform resolves this with a Server-to-Server Event Bridge: Authentication: When a client initiates a WebSocket connection (ws://localhost:3000/), it passes a JWT token in the connection URL parameters. The server verifies the token and adds the socket connection to a Project Room (project:${projectId}) after verifying project membership. The Emit Bridge: When a database change occurs, the API route makes a local HTTP POST request to /emit on the WebSocket server (secured by an EMIT_SECRET token). The WS server parses this and broadcasts the payload to all sockets in that project room.
The database is insulated from high-frequency queries using a dual-caching strategy (using Redis with in-memory fallback):

Project Stats Cache: Caches task counts, overdue counts, and dependency statistics for 5 minutes. Project Summary Cache: Caches metadata, latest snapshot dates, and critical paths. Team Stats Cache: Caches member roles and active user counts. Department Breakdown Cache: Caches task completions and critical path percentages grouped by department.

To maintain data consistency, specific database mutations invalidate target cache groups: Task Creation / Edit: Invalidates projectStats, projectHealth, and departmentBreakdown. CPM Run: Invalidates all project-related caches (stats, health, departments, summary). Team Assignment: Invalidates teamStats caches.
The diagram below maps the lifecycle of an incoming API request to edit a task duration:
This document provides a detailed technical reference for the Critical Path Method (CPM) scheduling engine, detailing both the native C++ engine (cpm_cli) and the JavaScript fallback engine.
The scheduling engine operates as a sequential transformation pipeline. Each phase acts on a directed project graph structure to calculate scheduling coordinates and isolate critical tasks.

Below is a comparison of how the graph nodes are structured in the two scheduling engines: C++ Structures (cpm_types.h) JavaScript Fallback Objects struct Task { std::string id; double duration; // Duration in days (can be fractional) DateTime early_start; // Calculated ES DateTime early_finish; // Calculated EF DateTime late_start; // Calculated LS DateTime late_finish; // Calculated LF double total_float; // TF (Slack in days) double free_float; // FF (Slack in days) bool is_critical; // True if total_float ≈ 0 }; struct Dependency { std::string predecessor_id; std::string successor_id; DependencyType type; // FS, SS, FF, SF double lag; // Lead/lag delay time LagUnit lag_unit; // DAYS, HOURS, WEEKS }; // Nodes mapped in route.ts const node = { id: task.id, duration: Number(task.duration), predecessors: [], successors: [], es: 0, ef: Number(task.duration), ls: 0, lf: 0, slack: 0, isCritical: false, indegree: 0 };

Before scheduling, the graph is validated to ensure it is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG): An in_degree counter map is initialized for all tasks. For each dependency, the successor's in_degree is incremented. Tasks with an in-degree of 0 (no predecessors) are added to a queue. While the queue is not empty, nodes are popped, successor in-degrees are decremented, and nodes hitting 0 in-degree are queued. If the count of processed nodes is less than the total node count, a cycle is detected and the run aborts.

Calculates Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF) in topological order: Initialize tasks: ES = project_start, EF = project_start + duration. For each task j, incoming dependencies from predecessors i are evaluated: Dependency Type Formula for Successor Earliest Start (ES_j) Finish-to-Start (FS) ES_j = max(ES_j, EF_i + lag) Start-to-Start (SS) ES_j = max(ES_j, ES_i + lag) Finish-to-Finish (FF) EF_j = max(EF_j, EF_i + lag) => ES_j = EF_j - duration_j Start-to-Finish (SF) EF_j = max(EF_j, ES_i + lag) => ES_j = EF_j - duration_j The overall project finish is the maximum earliest finish: project_finish = max(EF_k) for all tasks k

Calculates Latest Finish (LF) and Latest Start (LS) in reverse topological order: Initialize tasks: LF = project_finish, LS = project_finish - duration. For each task i, successors j are evaluated: Dependency Type Formula for Predecessor Latest Finish (LF_i) Finish-to-Start (FS) LF_i = min(LF_i, LS_j - lag) Start-to-Start (SS) LS_i = min(LS_i, LS_j - lag) => LF_i = LS_i + duration_i Finish-to-Finish (FF) LF_i = min(LF_i, LF_j - lag) Start-to-Finish (SF) LS_i = min(LS_i, LF_j - lag) => LF_i = LS_i + duration_i
Total Float is the amount of time a task can slide without delaying the project finish date: Total Float (TF) = LS - ES = LF - EF
Free Float is the amount of time a task can slide without delaying any successor task's earliest start: Free Float (FF) = max(0.0, min(ES_j) - EF_i) for all successors j of i

A task is classified as critical if its total float is approximately zero, evaluated using a small float threshold: IsCritical(Task) <=> |TF| < 1e-9 Find all Critical Start Tasks: critical tasks that have no critical predecessors. Run a Depth-First Search (DFS) from each critical start node, traversing only critical edges (edges connecting two critical tasks). If multiple parallel critical paths are discovered, the engine sums task durations along each path: Path Duration = sum(duration(t)) for all tasks t on path The path with the longest total duration is returned as the primary Critical Path.
This document details the database architecture, entity designs, schema relations, unique constraints, indexes, validation suites, and database performance benchmarks of the CPM platform.

Database Engine: PostgreSQL (v15+). Chosen for its strong ACID compliance, robust indexing support for nested relations, and support for high-throughput reads. ORM / Migration: Prisma. Provides a type-safe declarative schema syntax (schema.prisma) and automated migration generation.
The diagram below outlines the core tables, relationships, and roles in the database.

Workspace​ Purpose: Defines organizational boundaries. Fields: id (cuid, PK), name (string), slug (string, UK), createdAt (datetime), updatedAt (datetime). Constraints: @@unique([slug]) WorkspaceMember​ Purpose: Map users to workspaces with a base role. Fields: workspaceId (FK), userId (FK), role (OWNER, ADMIN, MEMBER, VIEWER). Constraints: @@id([workspaceId, userId]) (composite PK).

Project​ Purpose: Represents a project containing scheduling tasks and members. Fields: id (cuid, PK), workspaceId (FK), name, identifier (unique slug), startDate, targetDate, status (DRAFT, ACTIVE, DELAYED, COMPLETED), health (HEALTHY, WARNING, AT_RISK), ownerId (FK). Constraints: @@unique([workspaceId, identifier]). ProjectMember​ Purpose: Manages membership roles inside projects. Fields: projectId (FK), userId (FK), role (string), departmentId (FK), customRoleId (FK). Constraints: @@id([projectId, userId]).

Task​ Purpose: Represents an item of work with scheduling coordinates. Fields: id (custom string, PK), projectId (FK), title, description, duration (integer days), estimatedDays, startDate (calculated ES), endDate (calculated EF), state (BACKLOG, TODO, IN_PROGRESS, REVIEW, DONE, CANCELED), parentTaskId (self-relation FK). Indexes: @@index([projectId]) (fast loads of project tasks during CPM calculations). @@index([parentTaskId]) (parent hierarchy lookups). @@index([deletedAt]) (filters out soft-deleted items). Dependency​ Purpose: Connects predecessor tasks to successor tasks. Fields: id (cuid, PK), projectId (FK), predecessorTaskId (FK), successorTaskId (FK), dependencyType (FS, SS, FF, SF), lag (decimal), lagUnit (days, hours, weeks), strength (decimal stiffness). Constraints: @@unique([predecessorTaskId, successorTaskId, dependencyType]) (prevents redundant edges). Indexes: @@index([predecessorTaskId]) (forward pass traversal). @@index([successorTaskId]) (backward pass traversal). CPMSnapshot​ Purpose: Stores historical calculations computed by the C++ engine. Fields: id (cuid, PK), projectId (FK), version (string), calculationTime (datetime), projectDuration (decimal), criticalPath (JSON string array), payload (JSON task-by-task slack details). Indexes: @@index([projectId, version]) @@index([calculationTime])

To ensure database consistency, the repository includes verification scripts in database/tests/: Constraint Testing (database/tests/constraints.test.ts): Verifies unique violations (e.g., duplicate emails, workspace slug duplicates, identical dependencies). Referential Integrity Testing (database/tests/validation.test.ts): Asserts cascade deletions (e.g., deleting a project deletes all associated tasks, dependencies, and members). DAG Invariant Auditing: Checks that no self-referencing dependencies exist (predecessorTaskId != successorTaskId) and that durations are non-negative.

The database performance suite in scripts/ supports stress-testing and index optimization: Test Scripts: db_benchmark_runner.ts — Executes benchmark runs. db_benchmark_matrix.ts — Evaluates query latency on different graph shapes (chains, dense, tree). db_volume_benchmark.ts — Runs tests on small (100 tasks), medium (1,000 tasks), and large (5,000 tasks) projects. Key Database Optimizations: Indexing the projectId foreign key on the Task and Dependency tables is crucial for performance. Benchmarks show that without indexes, fetching dependencies for a 5,000-task graph requires a full table scan, degrading response times from 3ms to over 250ms.
This document describes the compilation process, build pipeline, and deployment configurations required to run the CPM platform in local and production environments.
The application is structured as a monorepo workspace. The build sequence is orchestrated by a single script in the root package.json: npm run build This command initiates a five-stage pipeline:

Prisma Generation: Compiles the schemas inside prisma/schema.prisma and writes type-safe query interfaces to @prisma/client in node_modules. Prisma Deployment: Applies pending database migrations (prisma/migrations/*) to the target PostgreSQL database instance. CMake Configuration: Reads CMake declarations inside CMakeLists.txt and prepares the native compilation workspace in /build. C++ Compilation: Compiles the source files inside engine/ into the native target executable /build/cpm_cli. Next.js Compilation: Runs the Webpack/Next.js compiler to bundle client-side React routes and bundle server-side API endpoints inside /apps/web/.next.

The following variables must be configured in the hosting environment (.env in development, or set directly in production env containers): Variable Name Required Default Value Description DATABASE_URL Yes None Connection string for the PostgreSQL database (e.g. postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/cpmdb). JWT_SECRET Yes super-secret-key-for-dev Cryptographic secret used to sign and verify user JWT sessions. EMIT_SECRET Yes dev-emit-secret Security token shared between API routes and the WebSocket HTTP bridge. REDIS_URL No None Connection string for Redis. If omitted, the server falls back to an in-memory cache. PORT No 3000 Port on which the unified server runs. INTERNAL_WS_SERVER_URL No http://localhost:3001 Address of the WebSocket server's HTTP bridge. NODE_ENV No development Environment mode (development or production).

The platform is designed to be deployed on platform-as-a-service (PaaS) providers. The following configuration defines a production container layout for Render (render.yaml): services: # 1. Main Unified Web & WebSocket Service - type: web name: cpm-platform env: node plan: starter buildCommand: npm install && npm run build startCommand: npm run start envVars: - key: NODE_ENV value: production - key: PORT value: 10000 - key: DATABASE_URL fromDatabase: name: cpm-postgres property: connectionString - key: REDIS_URL fromService: name: cpm-redis property: connectionString - key: JWT_SECRET generateValue: true - key: EMIT_SECRET generateValue: true # 2. Redis Cache Service - type: redis name: cpm-redis plan: free ipAllowList: [] # internal access only databases: # 3. PostgreSQL Database Instance - name: cpm-postgres plan: free databaseName: cpm_prod user: cpm_owner

Build Environment: Ensure the build server has a C++17 compatible compiler (e.g., g++ or clang) and cmake installed. On Render, these tools are available by default in the standard Node.js build images. Unified Port Routing: Render forwards both HTTP and WebSocket traffic through port 80/443 to the backend port specified by the PORT environment variable (e.g. 10000). Prisma Migrations: Running prisma migrate deploy during the build stage ensures schema migrations are applied before the new version of the web application boots, preventing schema-mismatch runtime errors.
This document provides a guide to setting up, developing, testing, and benchmarking the CPM platform on a local machine.

Before installing, ensure the following software is installed on the system: Node.js: version 20.0.0 or higher. npm: package manager (typically bundled with Node). C++ Compiler: g++ (v9+) or clang supporting C++17. CMake: version 3.10 or higher (for C++ compilation). PostgreSQL: database server (v15+). Redis: cache server (optional, falling back to local memory if absent).
Follow these steps to initialize the development environment:
# Install root workspace and subproject dependencies npm install
documents.s: Step 1: Clone the repository and install dependencies

documents.t: Create a .env file in the root directory: DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/cpm_dev?schema=public" JWT_SECRET="super-secret-key-for-dev-runs" EMIT_SECRET="dev-emit-secret" PORT=3000 INTERNAL_WS_SERVER_URL="http://localhost:3001"

Ensure your local PostgreSQL server is running, then deploy the schema: # Apply migrations to local PostgreSQL database npm run db:migrate # Seed database with sample workspaces, projects, and tasks npm run db:seed
documents.s: Step 3: Run Database Migrations and Seed

documents.t: Configure and compile the C++ CLI executable: # Run unified build scripts npm run build This generates the binary executable in /build/cpm_cli.
documents.s: Step 4: Compile the C++ Engine & Web Build

documents.t: Start the development servers (Next.js server on port 3000, and WebSocket server on port 3001): npm run dev Open http://localhost:3000 in the browser to view the application.
The repository includes validation tests to ensure database integrity and scheduling logic correctness.

Verify schema constraints, validations, transactions, and CRUD functionality: # Run validations and DAG checks npm run db:test:validation # Run constraint validation tests npm run db:test:constraints # Run CRUD operations tests npm run db:test:crud # Run transaction isolation tests npm run db:test:transaction
Native C++ unit tests verify scheduling logic calculations and edge cases: # Move to build folder and run tests cd build ctest
Stress-testing utilities are available in scripts/ to measure database performance under heavy loads:

Verify query performance under varying task graph sizes: # Small Test (100 tasks) npm run db:test:volume:small # Medium Test (1,000 tasks) npm run db:test:volume:medium # Large Test (5,000 tasks) npm run db:test:volume:large

Generate and visualize latency sweeps comparing performance on different graph shapes (chains, dense, tree): # Run latency tests and generate visual charts npm run db:bench:matrix:report This writes performance graphs and reports to the database/benchmarks/ directory.
To run the full suite of API and database benchmarks: npm run benchmarks:all
This document details the frontend architecture of the CPM platform, outlining page routes, client-side state, custom scheduling widgets, and layout design systems.
The frontend is a Next.js App Router application located in apps/web/src. Routing is split into Public and Authenticated route groups.

/ — Marketing Landing Page. Contains features lists, "How It Works" timeline, and an interactive multi-slide AI chat simulator. /login — User Sign-in card (Notion-styled card layout). /signup — Account Registration card.

/dashboard — General user workspace displaying recent projects, task completions checklist, upcoming milestones, and personal workload metrics. /notifications — Dedicated mailbox listing assignment notices, schedule changes, and approvals requests. /settings — User configuration page with toggle settings for AI Auto-Pilot/Scheduling modes and automation logs. /projects — List of all workspaces projects. /projects/[projectId] — Dynamic project view, using a common tabbed layout hosting six sub-views: ../overview — Main project KPI deck showing task distributions, active member counts, and project health. ../tasks — Searchable grid of project tasks with CRUD modals, bulk deletion, and bulk department assignment. ../gantt — Interactive time-bar timeline view. ../graph — React Flow-powered directed acyclic dependency network map. ../announcements — Pinned broadcast messages for project members. ../manage — User management pane for setting custom roles, invite codes, and updating project metadata.

The dependency graph renders task relationships in a Left-to-Right layout using @xyflow/react. Dagre Layout Engine: Client-side layout coordinates are calculated dynamically using the dagre library. It initializes an in-memory graph, sets node bounding boxes to 172x56px with direction LR, executes dagre.layout(), and assigns coordinates back to React Flow nodes. Node Styles: Nodes are color-coded based on their state (Todo: slate, In-Progress: yellow, Review: orange, Done: green, Overdue: red). Critical Path Highlighting: Tasks that are part of the critical path (where slack is exactly 0) are highlighted with a thick red border and a glowing shadow ring. Dependency edges are colored thick red and animated if they link two critical tasks. Tooltip: Hovering over a node displays a floating panel containing description text, task durations, and computed float times.

The Gantt page provides a custom grid scheduler without bulky calendar dependencies: Scales: Supports filtering by Days (48px columns), Weeks (12px per day), or Months (4px per day). Synchronized Scrolling: Splits the view into a sticky left task metadata table and a horizontal right scrolling timeline canvas, synchronized via CSS layouts. Visual Markers: Renders a vertical line marking today's date, shades weekends differently, and renders milestone tasks (0-day duration) as rotated black diamonds instead of wide rectangular bars. Progress Tracking: Sub-bars are drawn inside task bars displaying task completion percentages based on their status values (Done = 100%, In-Progress = 65%, Review = 90%).

The platform uses a warm, Notion-inspired aesthetic configured in Tailwind CSS v4 and globals.css: Canvas Backdrop: Clean off-white paper canvas background (#f6f5f4) to avoid screen fatigue. Action Elements: Standard black headlines with Notion Inter typography, utilizing tight tracking (letter-spacing) for large weights. A single deep blue (#0075de) is used for primary action links and focus states. Stickers & Badges: Small decorative panels use a playful sticker palette (sky-blue, light purple, pink, teal, green, orange) to classify modules without cluttering the primary chrome. Elevation: Tightly configured border-radii (radius-md: 8px for buttons, radius-lg: 12px for cards) and a 1px soft hairline divider (#e6e6e6) instead of heavy, dark drop-shadows.

Data inputs are validated client-side before sending payloads to backend routers: Libraries: react-hook-form manages form bindings and validation states, while zod defines data schemas. Task Forms: Validate that task durations are positive integers, titles are non-empty, and start/end dates are chronologically logical. Project Forms: Validate that identifier slugs contain only alphanumeric characters or hyphens (e.g. CPM-PROJ-A).

Asynchronous operations are managed by TanStack React Query: Cache Invalidation: Mutations (like creating a task, adding a dependency, or triggering a CPM run) invalidate query caches. This forces pages to refetch fresh datasets automatically. Query Keys: ['tasks', projectId] — Task list for a project. ['dependencies', projectId] — Project dependencies. ['cpmResults', projectId] — Latest snapshot computed by the C++ engine. ['project', projectId] — Project detail and metadata. ['departments', projectId] — Departments list.
This document provides definitions for domain concepts, architectural terms, and algorithms used in the CPM Project Management platform.
A project modeling technique used to calculate schedule flexibility and identify the sequence of dependent tasks that determines the minimum overall duration of a project.

The longest continuous sequence of dependent tasks in a project network. Any delay to a task on the critical path directly delays the project completion date. Tasks on the critical path have a Total Float equal to zero.
The earliest possible date or time a task can begin, calculated during the Forward Pass based on the finish times of all predecessor tasks.
The earliest possible date a task can be completed: EF = ES + duration
The latest date a task can complete without delaying the project completion date, calculated during the Backward Pass based on successor start times.
The latest date a task can begin without delaying the project completion date: LS = LF - duration
The amount of time a task can slide past its earliest start date without delaying the project completion date: TF = LS - ES = LF - EF
The amount of time a task can slide past its earliest start date without delaying the earliest start of any successor tasks: FF = min(ES_j) - EF (for all successors j of the task)
An offset delay required between a predecessor task and a successor task (e.g. waiting 3 days for concrete to dry before building walls). Can be configured as positive (delay) or negative (lead).
The successor task cannot start until the predecessor task completes. This is the default dependency type.
The successor task cannot start until the predecessor task has started.
The successor task cannot finish until the predecessor task has finished.
The successor task cannot finish until the predecessor task has started.
A directed graph containing no cycles. In this platform, tasks represent vertices and dependencies represent directed edges. The network must be a DAG so that scheduling coordinates can be computed.

A classical topological sorting algorithm. It works by finding nodes with an in-degree of 0, adding them to a sorted queue, removing their outgoing edges, and repeating. Used in the CPM engine to verify that project networks contain no cycles.
An Inter-Process Communication (IPC) pattern where a parent program (Node.js) starts a child program (C++ cpm_cli), writes data to its stdin stream, and parses results from its stdout stream.
Collision-resistant Unique Identifiers used as primary keys in the database (e.g. c1a2b3c4d5e6).
This document details the directories, subfolders, and files in the project repository workspace.

. ├── .agents/ # Agent environment skills configurations ├── apps/ │ └── web/ # Next.js web & WebSocket application │ ├── src/ │ │ ├── app/ # App Router pages and API routes │ │ ├── components/ # Client UI component library │ │ ├── lib/ # Authentication, database client, & cache layers │ │ └── store/ # Zustand global stores │ ├── package.json # Frontend package manifest │ ├── server.mjs # Next.js custom server configuration │ └── ws-server.mjs # Standing WebSocket room server ├── benchmarks/ # API load-testing and concurrency sweeps ├── build/ # Build workspace for the C++ compiler target ├── database/ # Schema documentation and validation scripts │ └── tests/ # Integration tests for validation & constraints ├── docs/ # Project documentation directory ├── engine/ # C++ Scheduling Engine Source Code │ ├── include/ # Header files (.h declarations) │ ├── src/ # Implementation source files (.cpp) │ └── tests/ # Native tests for scheduling logic ├── prisma/ # Database migrations, seeding, & schemas │ ├── migrations/ # Auto-generated SQL migration history │ ├── schema.prisma # Canonical database schema definitions │ └── seed.ts # Database seeding configuration ├── scripts/ # Database volume tests and benchmark runners ├── src/ # Integration code layers │ ├── database/ # Database repository interfaces │ └── integration/ # C++ engine client bindings (cpmService.ts) ├── CMakeLists.txt # CMake configuration script for cpm_cli compilation └── package.json # Monorepo root package manifest
Stores agents-specific instructions, skills (e.g., Supabase templates), and context locks. Used to onboard coding models and agents onto workspace-specific guidelines.

Houses the Next.js web application: src/app/: Implements folder-based App Routing. Defines page views, layouts, and REST API controller files under /api/v1/. src/components/: Houses reusable UI widgets (sidebar layouts, modals, buttons, input fields) styled with Tailwind CSS tokens. src/lib/: Houses shared infrastructure utilities, including JWT-signing algorithms (auth.ts), the global Prisma client wrapper (prisma.ts), and the Redis cache invalidation layer (project-overview-cache.ts). server.mjs & ws-server.mjs: Running these custom node configurations starts a unified HTTP web portal and WebSocket event bridge.

Houses the source code for the performance C++ critical path scheduler: include/: Defines structures like Task, Dependency, and ProjectGraph in cpm_types.h. src/: Contains core scheduling algorithms, including Cycle validation, Kahn's Topological Sort, Forward Pass, Backward Pass, Float Math, and DFS Path Finding. tests/: Native C++ test files to verify scheduling math.

Manages PostgreSQL schemas and deployments: schema.prisma: The source of truth for the database layout. migrations/: Automatically generated SQL scripts representing database migrations. seed.ts: Runs after initialization to insert project configurations, team members, tasks, and calculated snapshots.

Houses documents detailing the schema design and integration tests inside /tests (validations, cascade deletions, constraints, and transactions) to ensure PostgreSQL remains consistent during CRUD operations.
Contain tools to stress-test the application. They include scripts to generate synthetic graphs of up to 5,000 tasks and audit query response latencies.

Acts as the integration layer between backend services and database repositories: src/database/repositories.ts: Implements database queries and writes (Projects, Tasks, Dependencies, Snapshots), separating business logic from raw ORM syntax. src/integration/cpmService.ts: Maps database models to the JSON structures expected by the C++ engine, spawns the child process, and processes execution outputs.
This document details the state management architecture of the CPM platform, outlining how client-side UI states, server-cached data, and real-time WebSocket events are coordinated.

The application divides state into three distinct scopes: Transient UI State: Client-only states (e.g. open sidebars, active workspaces, zooming levels) managed by Zustand. Server Cache State: Remote database records (e.g. task titles, dependency links, CPM snapshots) cached locally by TanStack React Query. Real-time Broadcasts: Collaborative state synchronization (e.g. peer cursor movements, task updates, calculation completions) pushed via WebSockets.

Global UI state is managed by the Zustand store located in apps/web/src/store/workspaceStore.ts. Purpose: Tracks workspace navigation, active project contexts, search queries, sidebar toggles, and panel layouts. Why Zustand: Unlike React Context, Zustand does not trigger re-renders on components that consume unrelated slices of state. This is critical for performance in the React Flow graph view, where nodes must render smoothly without lag.

currentWorkspaceId (string | null): The active workspace context. currentProjectId (string | null): The project currently loaded in the viewer. sidebarOpen (boolean): Sidebar expansion toggle. searchQuery (string): Active search query. selectedTaskId (string | null): Task selected in the task detail sidebar.

Remote data fetching, caching, and cache invalidation are managed by TanStack React Query: Automatic Caching: Fetched tasks and dependencies are cached in memory. Subsequent navigation between tabs (e.g. switching from Tasks list to Gantt chart) reads from the query cache instantly without triggering redundant API requests. Background Refetching: Stale queries are refetched in the background when the user refocused the window.
documents.s: 3. Server State Caching via TanStack React Query

documents.t: When a user updates a task duration, a mutation is executed. Upon success, React Query invalidates related cache keys: const queryClient = useQueryClient(); const updateTaskMutation = useMutation({ mutationFn: async (updatedTask) => { const res = await fetch(`/api/v1/projects/${projectId}/tasks/${updatedTask.id}`, { method: 'PUT', body: JSON.stringify(updatedTask), }); return res.json(); }, onSuccess: (data) => { // Invalidate task queries so tables refetch fresh data queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['tasks', projectId] }); // Invalidate CPM results since task changes affect floats and dates queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['cpmResults', projectId] }); } });

To support real-time collaboration, a global WebSocketListener component wraps the authenticated layout: Connection: On page mount, the listener initiates a persistent WebSocket connection. Channel Subscription: When a project is loaded, the client sends a subscribe message: { "action": "join_room", "room": "project:c4b4a11f-db9f" } Incoming Event Handlers: task_created / task_updated / task_deleted: Triggers a query cache invalidation for ['tasks', projectId]. dependency_created / dependency_deleted: Triggers invalidations for both tasks and dependency cache keys. cpm_run_completed: Triggers a refetch of the scheduling results cache key ['cpmResults', projectId]. All client views (graph overlays, Gantt bars, slack badges) update automatically.
This document explains the technical choices made for the CPM platform's implementation.

Category Technology Selected Version Role / Purpose Frontend Core Next.js (App Router) 16.2.7 Page routing, server rendering, layout architecture. Frontend View React 19.2.4 Component-based interactive UI rendering. Graph Visualization React Flow (@xyflow/react) 12.11.0 Node-link layout and DAG visualization. Styling System Tailwind CSS / PostCSS v4 Design system styling utility tokens. State Management Zustand 5.0.14 Client-side routing and UI state stores. Data Fetching TanStack React Query 5.101.0 Async server-state synchronization & caching. Backend Core Node.js (custom server) v20+ / server.mjs REST endpoint routing and asset serving. Real-time Server WebSocket / ws 8.21.0 Real-time TCP event broadcast connection. Core Scheduling C++17 Compiler & CMake 3.10+ Native compiled high-speed CPM scheduler. ORM / Migration Prisma Client 6.4.1 Type-safe database queries and migrations. Database PostgreSQL v15+ Relational persistence storage. Caching Store Redis (ioredis) 5.11.1 In-memory cache store (with mock fallback). Auth Cryptography jose & argon2 6.2.3 / 0.44.0 JWT validation and password hashing. Date Arithmetic date-fns 4.4.0 Calendar parsing, formatting, and week checks.

Why: Next.js App Router provides hybrid rendering where public landing and auth pages are pre-rendered statically on the server, while project dashboards leverage client-side reactivity. React 19's async transitions simplify UI updates.

Why: Visualizing a network of dependencies is the core requirement of CPM. React Flow is the leading library for node-graph visualization, supporting panning, zooming, custom node containers, draggable coordinates, and edge markers out of the box.

Why: Tailwind CSS v4 provides a fast, utility-first styling pipeline that integrates with custom CSS variables. It enables the implementation of the Notion-inspired design system tokens (such as soft canvases, rounded buttons, and sticker accent cards) without writing verbose CSS class sheets.

Why: Zustand provides an extremely lightweight, hook-based global state manager. It is used here to share transient workspace states (such as active projects, open sidebar panels, and user context) across nested React Flow nodes and layout bars without context-re-render performance penalties.

Why: Scheduling calculations frequently invalidate data. React Query manages request deduplication, loading states, error boundaries, and cache invalidation policies, guaranteeing that when the CPM engine updates a project, all UI tables and charts update automatically.

Why: Standard serverless functions struggle to maintain persistent WebSocket connections. Running a unified Node.js server via server.mjs enables hosting the REST API, serving Next.js pages, and holding open WebSocket connections on the same port, simplifying containerization.
documents.s: Custom Node.js Web Server (server.mjs) & WebSocket Server (ws-server.mjs)

documents.t: Why: Scheduling algorithms (Topological Sorts, DFS, Forward/Backward Date passes) are CPU-bound and become highly inefficient in single-threaded JavaScript runtimes when scheduling graphs scale to thousands of nodes. A compiled C++ executable processes heavy graph operations in microseconds and is launched on-demand to safeguard Web server event loops from blocking.

Why: CPM schedules have highly relational structures (Tasks belong to Projects; Dependencies map tasks to other tasks; Members belong to Departments). PostgreSQL is a robust, ACID-compliant relational database. Prisma provides type-safe query building and auto-generates schema migrations.

Why: Multiple users viewing a project triggers high-frequency reads. Caching statistics, member lists, and critical paths in Redis avoids hitting the database on every route request. The custom cache wrapper falls back to an in-memory map if Redis goes offline, ensuring the application remains functional in minimalist dev environments.

Why: The system supports Manual, Assisted, Semi-Automatic, and Full Autopilot modes. This AI-driven proactive orchestration uses task analytics and risk scanning to suggest or automatically make scheduling adjustments (e.g. rescheduling tasks when a deadline-miss is predicted).

Why: To train and evaluate the deadline-miss prediction model, the repository includes a synthetic dataset generator (generate_dataset.py inside scripts) that models user productivity profiles ("College Student," "Freelancer," "Working Professional," "Entrepreneur"). It uses sequential LLM labeling pipelines with fault-tolerant backoff retry schedules to label productivity logs and generate realistic schedules.
This document details the core workflows of the CPM platform, mapping client interactions, database queries, background calculations, and real-time events.
This workflow handles user authentication, session creation using HTTP-only cookies, and session verification using React cache deduplication.

To reduce user churn, the dashboard implements a progressive initialization state machine based on account age and task counts: The get_dashboard_data service sends onboarding metadata (setup checklist completion percentages and current initialization stage) to customize the dashboard view.
This workflow handles creating tasks and updating scheduling networks, verifying that changes do not introduce circular dependencies.
Users can import existing schedules from ProjectLibre XML files:

Real-time synchronization ensures that when any project member updates a task, the changes are instantly reflected on all other members' active screens. Connection: On page mount, WebSocketListener.tsx connects to the WebSocket server using ws://localhost:3000?token=JWT_TOKEN. Subscription: The listener sends a join_project message containing the room identifier: project:${projectId}. The server validates project membership and adds the socket connection to the room. Broadcast: When a user modifies a task (e.g. changing status to IN_PROGRESS), the API route processes the database write and triggers an emit request. The WebSocket server pushes this change to all other clients subscribed to the project room, triggering an automatic cache invalidation and UI refresh.
